lesson.learn:
Natural selection is the process by which traits become more common because they help organisms survive and reproduce in a specific environment. It is not a conscious force. It is the result of individuals with different traits leaving different numbers of offspring over time.

That process explains how populations adapt without needing a planner. If some variants are better suited to the environment, they tend to persist. Over many generations, that slowly changes the population's average traits.
lesson.simpler: Natural selection means helpful inherited traits become more common because the organisms with them leave more offspring.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Selection is about differential survival and reproduction across generations.
lesson.deeperDive:
For selection to work, variation has to be inherited, and the environment has to favor some variants over others. The classic peppered moth example shows how a change in conditions can change which form is more successful. Darwin's finches show how different environments can favor different beak shapes. The result is not perfection, just relative advantage.

Natural selection is slow when generation times are long and fast when generation times are short. That is why the same evolutionary logic can shape bacteria, birds, and humans, even though the timescales look very different.
lesson.funFact: Natural selection has no plan; it only keeps what works better right now.

people: Field naturalists - people who observe selection in the wild
places: Industrial England - a historic selection example
The Galápagos Islands - a famous adaptation setting
Laboratories and field stations - where selection is studied
years: Late 20th century - selection is observed in real time
Present day - selection is central to biology
concepts: Adaptation - a trait favored over time
Variation - differences that selection can work on
